title: How knowledge of cycling practice and changes to the British Highway Code relate to perceptions of cyclist discourtesy
abstract:
In 2022 the UK Highway Code had a major update including advice for cyclists around lane positioning that reflects existing safety training practice and guidance for drivers to leave at least 1.5 m of lateral clearance when overtaking cyclists. This study uses a repeated measures design to examine the impact of changes to the Highway Code on knowledge of cycling road positioning practice, and the effect of this knowledge on perceptions of cyclist discourtesy.

Knowledge of cyclist positioning practice improved significantly between the two surveys. A hierarchical multiple regression found that cyclist positioning knowledge score added significantly to the model predicting perceptions of cyclist discourtesy, with the change in R2 between the two models being significant. A separate hierarchical multiple regression, with cyclist positioning knowledge as the DV and Highway Code knowledge as the additional stage two IV, was not significant.

The findings showed that while the highway code changes did not predict improvements in knowledge, drivers with better knowledge did have more positive opinions of cyclist behaviour, when controlling for their own participation in cycling.

This was a novel study in applying a repeated measures design before and after the changes to the UK Highway Code. The findings will help inform development of interventions designed to improve road safety for cyclists.
text:
How knowledge of cycling practice and changes to the British 
Highway Code relate to perceptions of cyclist discourtesy
William Cubbin a,*
, Kjell van Paridon a, Matthew Timmis a, Helen Keyes b
a Cambridge Centre for Sport and Exercise Science, Anglia Ruskin University, Cambridge, Cambridgeshire, United Kingdom
b School of Psychology, Sport and Sensory Sciences, Anglia Ruskin University, Cambridge, United Kingdom
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Cyclists
Drivers
Perceived norms
Highway code
Attitudes
Road positioning
A B S T R A C T
In 2022 the UK Highway Code had a major update including advice for cyclists around lane 
positioning that reflects existing safety training practice and guidance for drivers to leave at least 
1.5 m of lateral clearance when overtaking cyclists. This study uses a repeated measures design to 
examine the impact of changes to the Highway Code on knowledge of cycling road positioning 
practice, and the effect of this knowledge on perceptions of cyclist discourtesy.
Knowledge of cyclist positioning practice improved significantly between the two surveys. A 
hierarchical multiple regression found that cyclist positioning knowledge score added signifi­
cantly to the model predicting perceptions of cyclist discourtesy, with the change in R2 between 
the two models being significant. A separate hierarchical multiple regression, with cyclist posi­
tioning knowledge as the DV and Highway Code knowledge as the additional stage two IV, was 
not significant.
The findings showed that while the highway code changes did not predict improvements in 
knowledge, drivers with better knowledge did have more positive opinions of cyclist behaviour, 
when controlling for their own participation in cycling.
This was a novel study in applying a repeated measures design before and after the changes to 
the UK Highway Code. The findings will help inform development of interventions designed to 
improve road safety for cyclists.
1. Introduction
Driver aggression and risky manoeuvres around cyclists have been shown to be a barrier for many people to travel by bicycle 
(Davies et al., 1997) and creates a collision risk for those people who do cycle (Aldred 2016). Links have been made between driver rule 
knowledge and more positive attitudes towards cyclists (Rissel et al., 2002). The current research explores this link further by looking 
at how knowledge of formal guidance for cyclists could support more positive interpretations of cyclist behaviour by drivers.
The UK Highway Code (Department for Transport, 2022) contains both advice and legally enforceable rules for all users of public 
roads in the UK. In January 2022 some major changes to the Highway Code were published. This included advice to leave at least 1.5 m 
of lateral clearance when overtaking cyclists at speeds of up to 30mph, and more space at higher speeds. It also gave more recognition 
to aspects of training that have been delivered nationally under the Bikeability cyclist training scheme since 2007 (The Bikeability 
Trust, 2019). The Bikeability lane position training now endorsed by the Highway Code includes never to ride at the very edge of the 
* Corresponding author.
E-mail address: wjc116@pgr.aru.ac.uk (W. Cubbin).

Psychology and Behaviour
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/trf
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trf.2025.103450

Transportation Research Part F: Psychology and Behaviour 117 (2026) 103450

( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ ). 
road on the road shoulder or in the gutter. Instead cyclists are to take a Left Lane position at least 0.5 m from the kerb when it is safe for 
following traffic to overtake, and to ride in a Centre Lane position in slow traffic and when overtaking would be unsafe.
The Highway Code update placed a new expectation on motorists in relation to how they interact with cyclists. A YouGov survey 
commissioned by Cycling UK (YouGov, 2022) found that 90 % of drivers could recall that there had been some recent changes to the 
highway code. However this survey also found 66 % of the drivers who were aware changes had been made did not know that cyclists 
should position themselves in the centre of the lane on quiet roads and in slow-moving traffic. Furthermore 70 % of the drivers who 
were aware changes had been made did not know that drivers should leave at least 1.5 m of space when overtaking cyclists. This survey 
required participants to make the correct selection on multiple choice questions, so it did not require participants to recall the rules 
verbatim. Respondents were encouraged not to guess and had the option to indicate they didn’t know the answer. However the 
multiple choices on offer could have allowed some respondents to make a correct educated guess by selecting the most plausible 
option, so may slightly overestimate the level of Highway Code knowledge.
The aim of the current study is to evaluate whether these changes in the Highway Code influenced drivers’ perceptions of cyclists’ 
road positioning, and whether drivers who do have this knowledge make different interpretations of cyclist behaviour.
1.1. Behaviour towards cyclists
Cycle training schemes The Bikeability Trust (2019), Cycling 4 Safety (2022), The Center for Cycling Education (2021) and the UK 
Highway Code (Department for Transport, 2022) encourage cycling in a centre lane position in certain scenarios, and at least 0.5 m 
from the kerb at other times. This guidance is designed to discourage overtaking in situations where to do so would be risky. However, 
both Rubie et al. (2020) and Mackenzie, Dutschke and Ponte (2021) found that riding further from the kerb was related to smaller 
passing distances. This demonstrates a conflict between official guidance on safe and responsible cycling and how drivers react to 
cyclists who are following this official guidance. The experimental results indicating closer passing distances when cycling further from 
the kerb are supported by Aldred (2016). This study found many riders who were aware of the advice to ride in the centre of their lane 
were nevertheless unwilling to do so for fear of provoking an angry or impatient response from motorists.
There is a methodological limitation with using lateral passing distance to evaluate the effect of rider positioning, in that it is unable 
to count potentially risky passes that did not happen because the positioning of the cyclist successfully discouraged the driver from 
attempting an overtake in the first place. Rasch et al. (2020) provides evidence that riding further from the kerb can prevent overtakes 
in situations where safety margins are smaller. The study, conducted in Sweden on a test track found that when a robot cyclist was in a 
centre-lane position, 50 % of drivers waited for an oncoming vehicle to pass before overtaking, compared to 29 % when the robot 
cyclist was closer to the kerb.
Other studies have examined factors that may influence driver attitudes to cyclists. These studies have not quantified how these 
factors affect lateral passing distance but do identify factors that could influence drivers’ decisions when contemplating a passing 
manoeuvre where lateral clearance is minimal. For example Basford et al. (2002) found that drivers recognised the need to take care 
around cyclists but this was somewhat counteracted by perceived social pressure from other drivers. This study also found that 
infrastructure that failed to give clear ownership of space or prompted improvised driver responses, was associated with drivers 
perceiving cyclists as less courteous as well as drivers being more likely to execute manoeuvres that could be alarming to cyclists. 
Heesch et al. (2011) found a range of factors that were associated with cyclists recalling harassment from motorists while riding. These 
factors comprised being younger, less obese, having more than 2 years’ experience cycling, higher cycling frequency, living in socio- 
economically advantaged areas, cycling for recreation and cycling for competition. Gender was not linked to levels of harassment. 
These factors conceivably correlate with young, fit cycling enthusiasts attracting more harassment than people cycling for utility 
purposes or out of economic necessity. A possible reason for this could be a perception that cycling for sport is optional while cycling 
for transport is necessary, so the source of any inconvenience experienced when encountering sport cyclists may be seen as less 
legitimate than when encountering utility cyclists. Shinar (1998) suggests drivers are more likely to become angry when delayed if the 
source of the delay is viewed as illegitimate.
1.2. Knowledge, attitude and behaviour
Drivers who also ride bicycles (cyclist-drivers) have been shown to have a more positive attitude towards cyclists than drivers who 
don’t cycle, with in-group bias being discussed as likely to underpin the more positive attitude towards cyclists (Fruhen and Flin, 
2015). In addition to in-group bias, more positive attitudes towards cyclists are also associated with higher levels of general road rule 
knowledge (Rissel et al., 2002). Oldmeadow et al. (2019) gives more insight into this relationship by demonstrating that general road 
rule knowledge is related to greater perceived legitimacy of cyclists as road users, and these higher levels of perceived legitimacy are 
associated with lower levels of driver anger. Johnson et al., (2014) found cyclist-drivers were 1.5 times more likely than non-cyclists to 
report safe driving behaviours when overtaking cyclists, had better road rule knowledge, were less likely to report frustration when 
wanting to overtake a cyclist and were less likely to report that cyclists are unpredictable. This unpredictability of cyclists has been 
found to be a source of irritation among drivers (Basford et al., 2002). These studies indicate that negative attitudes towards cyclists 
can be mediated by road rule knowledge by making cyclists seem more legitimate and predictable.
The Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) (Ajzen, 1991) posits that behavioural intentions are underpinned by beliefs and attitudes 
towards the behaviour, beliefs around subjective norms of behaviour and beliefs around one’s ability to exhibit the behaviour. 
Negative attitudes towards cyclists have been linked to more aggressive driving behaviour around them (Fruhen and Flin, 2015). The 
attitude of a driver towards safe overtaking of a cyclist may therefore be influenced by their attitude towards the cyclist. This 
W. Cubbin et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                        
Transportation Research Part F: Psychology and Behaviour 117 (2026) 103450 
2 
conceptual link between attitudes towards cyclists and attitudes towards driving behaviour when encountering them is illustrated in 
Fig. 1:
1.3. Importance of perspective
It is important to consider whether participant responses can be skewed by the perspective from which stimuli are presented. For 
example the fundamental attribution error (or self-serving bias) is the tendency for people to blame their own errors on external factors 
and credit their successes to their own virtues. This was demonstrated in a study that found people who were given the role of an 
participant in a scenario exhibited self-serving bias while those who were observers did not (Campbell and Sedikides, 1999). Similarly, 
the actor-observer bias is the tendency to attribute other people’s errors to character flaws but one’s own errors to external causes. This 
is shown in a study that found when inconvenienced by another road user, people have a tendency to blame the other road user’s 
behaviour on personality flaws and underestimate the extent to which they were responding to external factors (Wickens, Mann and 
Wiesenthal, 2013). If a driver is inconvenienced by behaviour interpreted as symptomatic of a character flaw, they are more likely to 
direct an angry response towards the offending road user than if the exact same behaviour was interpreted as being in line with ex­
pected norms. This creates a situation where a driving manoeuvre that poses a risk to a cyclist may be seen as justifiable by the driver, if 
the cyclist’s behaviour is at odds with the expected norm. A lack of knowledge around how cyclists are taught to interact with their 
environment may increase the likelihood of cyclist’s behaviour being interpreted as subverting the expected norms, reducing the 
driver’s inhibition towards the risky manoeuvre.
Knowledge among drivers of recommended cyclist road positioning practice was found to be a mediating factor in perceptions of 
cyclists’ liability in footage of close passes by motor vehicles (Cubbin et al., 2024). The stimuli used in that study were filmed from the 
perspective of the cyclist experiencing the close pass. Participants were asked questions to measure the extent to which they believed 
the cyclist bore some responsibility for the close pass. As they were viewing the pass from the perspective of the cyclist, the actor- 
observer bias suggests survey participants would interpret the situation as though they were the cyclist (actor), rather than as an 
independent observer or from the perspective of the motor vehicle driver. The current study shifts this perspective to that of a driver 
approaching a cyclist from behind. This will frame the cyclist as being distinct from the actor in the scenario, meaning any actor- 
observer or self-serving bias will be aligned with the experience of drivers when they encounter cyclists on the road. This will 
minimise uncertainty in how the results might apply to drivers in real world situations.
1.4. Synthesis
Infrastructure can give clear signals to drivers on ownership of space that will positively or negatively affect their attitudes towards 
the cyclists they overtake Basford et al. (2002). However it appears that knowledge of road rules around cycling can also influence 
these encounters (Oldmeadow et al., 2019). Knowledge that allows a driver to understand cyclists’ positioning choices, and even their 
choice to be on the road in the first place, may help overcome the fundamental attribution error – in this case attributing their 
frustration at being delayed to a personality or behavioural flaw in the cyclist. Furthermore, greater cycling-specific knowledge may 
support more positive attitudes towards cyclists by helping drivers become better at predicting cyclist behaviour and being more likely 
to view that behaviour as being consistent with the expected norm.
Fig. 1. Logic model linking knowledge of cyclist positioning with behaviour around cyclists, and how it links to the Theory of Planned Behaviour.
W. Cubbin et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                        
Transportation Research Part F: Psychology and Behaviour 117 (2026) 103450 
3 
1.5. Hypotheses
Existing research suggests improved knowledge among drivers of cyclist positioning is related to increased perceptions of cyclist 
legitimacy, and this perceived legitimacy could influence greater lateral clearance afforded to cyclists when overtaking. Changes to the 
UK Highway Code and accompanying publicity may have the potential to increase the level of knowledge among UK drivers of cyclist 
positioning practice, leading to the following hypotheses: 
H1: People with a better understanding of cyclist positioning perceive cyclists as being less discourteous above and beyond per­
ceptions related to how much their lifestyle involves driving, level of driving experience, cycling frequency and road user identity.
H2: The updates to the Highway Code have improved general knowledge of the most appropriate cyclist positioning in various 
scenarios.
1.6. Research questions and objectives
Our study builds upon the work of Cubbin et al. (2024) which found that knowledge of recommended cyclist road-positioning 
among UK drivers was a mediating factor that predicted lower levels of liability apportioned to cyclists in close passes while con­
trolling for Driving Purposes (DP) and road user identity (RUI). DP measures the number of purposes for which a person uses a car, it is 
not a measure of driving dependence as it does not account for availability or willingness to use alternatives, but it does show the extent 
to which a participant’s lifestyle involves driving. RUI is a 100 point scale where participants can place themselves, ranging from 0 =
“I’m more of a driver” to 100 = “I’m more of a cyclist”.
The following research questions have been formulated to determine if updates to the Highway Code have improved driver un­
derstanding of cyclist road positioning and if variations in this level of understanding are related to general perceptions of cyclist 
discourtesy among drivers:
Research question 1: Does greater knowledge of cyclist positioning significantly predict perceptions of cyclist legitimacy above 
and beyond the predictive ability of DP, driving experience, cycling frequency and RUI?
Research question 2: Has the level of knowledge around cyclist positioning improved since the January 2022 Highway Code 
update, and can any change be attributed to the highway code changes?
The objectives of this research aim to evaluate the impact on driver attitudes to cyclists resulting from changes made in 2022 to the 
UK Highway Code advice for cyclists. It further aims to determine if drivers who are better able to select appropriate lane positions for 
cyclists, have more positive attitudes towards cyclists in general. It will analyse the relationship between Highway Code knowledge 
and any changes in the ability of a within-subjects group of study participants to select appropriate lane positions for cyclists. It will 
also analyse the relationship between the ability to select appropriate lane positions and general attitudes towards cyclists, while 
controlling for potential in group and out group biases. This is to determine if improved cycling specific knowledge can predict more 
positive attitudes to cyclists, and whether the changes to the Highway Code have had any effect on this.
2. Material and methods
2.1. Participants
A survey of UK drivers was carried out in August 2020 to investigate opinions on appropriate cyclist road positioning across a range 
of road environments and driver characteristics. This survey collected information on which position within the carriageway par­
ticipants thought was most appropriate for a cyclist to adopt, as well as information on participants’ experience of driving and cycling 
and their self-reported tendencies for how they react when something makes them angry while driving. Participants were recruited via 
the Prolific survey platform which pays UK minimum wage pro-rata for time taken to complete surveys.

Transportation Research Part F: Psychology and Behaviour 117 (2026) 103450 
4 
When the Highway Code was updated in 2022 it created an opportunity to re-run the original questionnaire to study whether 
participants’ beliefs around cyclist road positioning had changed following the Highway Code update. Participants who took part in 
the August 2020 survey, that were still active users of the Prolific platform in 2023, were invited to complete a second iteration of the 
survey to allow a repeated measures analysis to be carried out. This approach allowed their responses to be compared before and after 
changes were made to the Highway Code with its related publicity campaigns and media coverage. The demographics of the par­
ticipants are summarised in Table 1.
2.2. Stimuli
The survey questions are summarised in Table 2 and included multiple choice questions depicting 13 different scenarios, each 
showing a picture of a female cyclist from the perspective of a motorist approaching from behind, where the road environment and 
rider-to-kerb distance were varied. Each scenario was repeated at three different distances to the camera making 39 questions in total. 
One example is shown in in Fig. 2.
The cyclist was wearing casual clothing, a helmet and a yellow hi-vis vest. In each set of three pictures the rider was shown on the 
same road at the same distance, but variously positioned immediately adjacent to the kerb, around 0.5–1.0 m from the kerb, and in the 
centre of the lane. The 13 scenarios were chosen to represent situations where a rider should be in a more central position due to road 
narrowing caused by parked cars or unsafe overtaking conditions caused by bends, as well as scenarios where it would be appropriate 
for a cyclist to take a left lane position to help a following vehicle to overtake. These scenarios are outlined in Table 3:
The participants were prompted to select which road position they believed to be most appropriate for the cyclist to take, these 
were either A – “Shoulder” (on the road shoulder or in the gutter), B – “Left Lane” position around 0.5–1.0 m from the kerb or C – 
“Centre Lane” position (in the centre of the lane or close to the centre of the road for a single lane road). Bikeability training and the 
Highway Code both teach that position A is never appropriate, with position B being appropriate where it is safe for a following vehicle 
to overtake and position C being appropriate where the cyclist is travelling close to motor vehicle speeds or where traffic, road layout 
or other factors mean it is not safe for a following vehicle to overtake.
Bikeability and Highway Code guidance defines position A (shoulder) as never being correct, so any answer giving position A was

5 
given zero points. Guidance was sought from two Bikeability Training Managers who advised that defining the ‘correct’ position 
involves an element of subjectivity and can also depend on factors that cannot be shown in a photo, such as rider speed. In many 
situations the Bikeability Training Manager guidance deemed B as slightly better than C (or vice versa), but neither being categorically 
wrong, so both options were made available. By answering B or C respondents demonstrated the knowledge that A was incorrect so an 
answer of B or C were both given a score of 1 point.
2.3. Measures and analysis
Research Question 1: “Does greater knowledge of cyclist positioning significantly predict perceptions of cyclist legitimacy above and beyond 
the predictive ability of DP, driving experience, cycling frequency and RUI?”
To answer this research question a test was formulated for hypothesis #1 (that people with a better understanding of cyclist 
positioning perceive cyclists as being less discourteous above and beyond perceptions related to how much their lifestyle involves 
driving, level of driving experience, cycling frequency and road user identity) by applying a bootstrapped hierarchical multiple 
regression to determine if cycling position knowledge significantly predicts perceptions of cyclist discourtesy. The regression included 
variables identified in Cubbin et al. (2024) that significantly predicted measures of perceived liability for cyclists in video clips of close- 
pass overtakes by motor vehicles, they comprise:
Driving Purposes: This is a count of the number of different driving purposes the participant regularly engages in. Participants 
were asked to select all of the following statements that were applicable to them; ‘I drive for all or part of my job’, ‘I drive to get to my 
place of work or study’, ‘I drive for personal business such as shopping’, ‘I drive to attend social occasions or leisure activities’, ‘Driving 
is a leisure activity for me’. The DP score was derived by scoring 1 point for each of the driving purpose types selected. This approach 
infers the extent to which a respondent’s lifestyle involves driving by focussing on purposes rather than mileage or frequency and was 
used in Cubbin et al. (2024). The approach was adapted from established practice in using driving purpose categories to measure car 
dependence from UK Department for Transport National Travel Survey data (Lucas, 2009).
Years licence held: As a proxy measure of driving experience. This is an established measure used in multiple studies included in a 
meta-analysis of driver experience (Robbins and Chapman, 2019).
Frequency riding bicycle on road: Selected from the following options; Never or almost never, A few times a year, At least once a 
month, At least once a week.
Road-user self-identity (RUI): As measured on a sliding scale from 0 (100 % driver) to 100 (100 % cyclist).
Research Question 2: “Has the level of knowledge around cyclist positioning improved since the January 2022 Highway Code update, and 
can any change be attributed to the highway code changes?”
To answer this research question a test was formulated for hypothesis #2 (that the updates to the Highway Code have improved 
general knowledge of the most appropriate cyclist positioning in various scenarios) by using a t-test to compare changes in applied

6 
knowledge of cyclist positioning and then a bootstrapped hierarchical multiple regression to determine if these changes can be 
attributed to the Highway Code updates.
For participants who took part in both surveys, a t-test was used to compare changes between the two surveys in the proportion of 
answers indicating they believed that A (tight to the kerb) was the most appropriate position for the cyclist to take. This was to 
determine if there has been a change in respondents’ understanding of cyclist positioning, where a reduction in the proportion of 
answers stating position A is most appropriate would indicate an improvement in knowledge.
Two distinct measures were made in relation to the Highway Code; Highway Code recollection was measured by answers to 
questions on when the most recent major update to the Highway Code was made. Highway Code knowledge was measured by accuracy 
in answering questions on Highway Code advice for cyclist positioning.
A bootstrapped hierarchical multiple regression was carried out to determine if there was a relationship between variables recorded 
in the survey, including scores on Highway Code knowledge and recollection of the changes, and the level of improvement on the 
cyclist positioning questions between the two surveys. Fig. 3 shows how the planned analyses realte to the data collected in both stages 
of the study.
3. Results
The first iteration of the survey conducted in 2020 received 421 complete responses that were included for analysis. Of these 421 
respondents, 167 completed the second iteration of the survey in 2023.
Table 4 summarises cyclist positional knowledge scores for all the participants in both surveys. This shows that at least 52.2 % of 
responses in 2020 showed a lack of understanding of Bikeability advice on cyclist positioning for the scenario depicted. Most of the 
remaining responses were more conservative in the amount of space they afforded the cyclist by selecting B (left lane) rather than C 
(centre lane). The subset of participants in survey 1 who also took part in the second survey, allowed even less space to cyclists with 
over 53 % of responses selecting the A (tight to kerb) position. The same participants showed improved knowledge in the second survey 
with only 38.7 % selecting the A position and twice as many answers indicating the C position than they did on the first survey.
A paired samples t-test showed a significant increase in cyclist position knowledge score between survey 1 and survey 2 among 
participants who completed both surveys t(166) = -6.445, p < 0.001.
3.1. Results for RQ1 “Does greater knowledge of cyclist positioning significantly predict perceptions of cyclist legitimacy above and beyond 
the predictive ability of DP, driving experience, cycling frequency and RUI?
A simple exploratory correlation was conducted between the variables measured in the survey using Pearson correlation. The 
Pearson coefficients in Table 5 show a significant negative correlation between perceptions of cyclist discourtesy and RUI as a cyclist (r 
= -0.272, p < 0.01) and frequency cycling (r = -0.308, p < 0.01). There is a significant positive correlation between perceptions of 
cyclist discourtesy and age (r = 0.275, p < 0.01) and years licence held (r = 0.279, p < 0.01). Age and years licence held are strongly 
co-correlated (r = 0.948, p < 0.01). There is not a significant correlation between perceptions of cyclist discourtesy and cyclist position 
scores.
The relationship between scores on cyclist positioning knowledge and perceptions of cyclist discourtesy was tested using a 
Fig. 3. Analysis block diagram.
W. Cubbin et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                        
Transportation Research Part F: Psychology and Behaviour 117 (2026) 103450 
7 
bootstrapped hierarchical multiple regression, summarised in Table 6. Variance inflation factors ranged between 1.033 and 2.129 
indicating multicollinearity did not affect the model.
Both models significantly predicted the DV of cyclist discourtesy; F(6, 148) = 6.131, p < 0.001, R2 = 0.199, and F(7, 147) = 6.118, 
p < 0.001, R2 = 0.226 (see Table 6). Three of the five variables added significantly to the prediction; Years licence held, (β = 0.273, t =
3.640, p < 0.001), Frequency cycling (β = -0.249, t = -2.360, p = 0.020), and stage 2 positioning score (β = -0.175, t = -2.244, p =
0.026). Highway Code knowledge did not make a significant contribution, indicating that efforts to improve knowledge of cyclist 
positioning through publicised updates to the highway code have not translated to lower perceptions of cyclist discourtesy.
The change in R2 between the two models was significant (ΔR2 = 0.027, Fchange = 0.026), indicating that when controlling for 
participation in cycling and driving experience, people with greater knowledge of cyclist positioning practice tend to have lower 
perceptions of cyclist discourtesy.
3.2. Results for RQ2 “Has the level of knowledge around cyclist positioning improved since the January 2022 Highway Code update, and 
can any change be attributed to the highway code changes?”
A t-test showed a statistically significant change in the cyclist positioning scores between the two stages of the survey (t(166) =
-6.445, p < 0.001).
The coefficients in Table 5 indicate a significant positive relationship between cyclist positioning score on the second survey and 
Highway Code knowledge (r = 0.328, p < 0.01). A smaller but still significant positive relationship was present when looking at how 
much scores improved between the two surveys and Highway Code knowledge (r = 0.165 p < 0.05). There was also a positive cor­
relation between Highway Code knowledge and cyclist positioning scores on the first survey (r = 0.171, p < 0.05) that took place 
before the highway code was updated.
A bootstrapped hierarchical multiple regression was carried out to determine the effect of highway code knowledge on the changes 
between the two surveys in cyclist positional knowledge, while controlling for potentially confounding variables. An ANOVA approach 
was used as this method is robust to non-normally distributed data when the group sizes are equal (Field, 2009, p.360). Table 7 shows 
this model was not significant; F(7, 147) = 1.178, p = 0.319, R2 = 0.053.
As neither model is significant it demonstrates that the highway code knowledge variable does not predict changes in cyclist road 
positioning score.
4. Discussion
4.1. Key findings
It has been previously established that negative attitudes and behaviours towards cyclists by some drivers is a major barrier 
preventing many people from using bicycles for transport, restricting the wide-ranging benefits brought to society by a move from 
motorised to pedal powered transport. The aim of this study was to determine whether greater knowledge of recommended cyclist road 
positioning practice can predict more favourable interpretations of cyclist behaviour among drivers. It also examined whether changes 
to the Highway Code, applied and publicised in January 2022, can be linked to any improvement in understanding of cyclist 
positioning.
In line with our hypothesis, greater knowledge of cyclist positioning practice predicts lower perceptions of cyclist discourtesy. This 
model controls for potentially confounding factors that may affect whether an individual is positively or negatively disposed towards 
cyclists, comprising how much their lifestyle involves driving, years licence held, how they identify as a road user (driver / cyclist) and 
how frequently they cycle on the road themselves. This finding implies that increasing driver knowledge of cyclist positioning may 
enable a more positive interpretation of observed cyclist behaviour which could lead to a more favourable opinion of cyclists overall. A 
more favourable opinion of cyclists among drivers could help create a less hostile environment for people to travel by bicycle.
Within the cohort that took part in both stages of this study there was a significant improvement between 2020 and 2023 in scores 
for knowledge of appropriate road positioning for cyclists. Pearson corelations showed that Highway Code knowledge and recollection 
were significantly correlated with the improvement in positioning scores between the two iterations of the survey. However, a 
bootstrapped hierarchical multiple regression model using change in cyclist positioning score as the dependent variable, and Highway 
Code knowledge as the additional second stage independent variable, was not significant. This showed that highway code knowledge 
could not predict the change in cyclist positioning score when controlling for Frequency cycling, Highway Code recollection, Driving

9 
Purposes, years licence held, perceived cyclist discourtesy and road user identity (cyclist / driver). The fact the Highway Code 
knowledge did not predict scores in selecting the appropriate position for cyclists suggests this knowledge alone has limited or 
negligible relevance, with drivers needing a more practical understanding of the guidance to understand how it applies to on-road 
scenarios. Some of this practical understanding may have been evident in the second stage of the survey as it took place after the 
COVID-19 lockdowns. The lockdowns would have given participants the opportunity to get used to the elevated numbers of cyclists on 
the roads (Department for Transport, 2021), or have spent some time themselves cycling on the road – building first-hand experience of 
how cyclists interact with their environment.
The variables included in the model do not explain the reason for the improved cyclist positioning scores, but the improvement in 
scores relating to perceptions of cyclist discourtesy indicate the impact of this improvement could support reduced hostility towards 
cyclists. The data examined for this study indicates that simply being a few years older and more experienced is unlikely to be an 
explanation for this improvement because years licence held did not contribute significantly to the model.
One reason for the improvement in scores could be an indirect effect of the Highway Code being disseminated via word-of-mouth 
where the precise rules are lost but the general spirit of the guidance is retained. Conflict between cyclists and motorists is a prominent 
current affairs issue in the UK, for example featuring as a half hour special on the BBC prime time current affairs programme Panorama 
in November 2022 (’Road Rage: Cars v Bikes’, 2022). This creates much discourse around the issue giving opportunities for infor­
mation to spread. This could include information around cyclist positioning practice being shared independently of the Highway Code 
changes. Another possibility is that the participants may have subconsciously picked up on the changes more easily, having previously 
answered questions on the topic in stage 1 of the survey.

4.2. Limitations
The mean age of participants in this study was 39.56, over 10 years younger than the average licence holder (Department for 
Transport, 2024) and almost 10 years younger than the average for the eligible population aged 18+ (Nomis, 2023). These variables 
were controlled for in the analysis but this may limit how well the results can be applied to the wider population.
Highway code knowledge was measured using a multiple-choice question and a graphical sliding scale. The options given in the 
questions may assist participants in making an educated guess as to the correct answer. This may lead to an overestimate of actual 
highway code knowledge among the sample.
4.3. Further work
The current study found that knowledge of cyclist positioning was a significant component in variations in perceptions of cyclist 
discourtesy. Further work is needed to understand how best to drive increases in knowledge of correct cyclist positioning.
Published literature discussed in this paper supports the legitimisation of cyclists as a measure that has potential to create more 
courtesy shown towards cyclists (Rissel et al.,2002, Oldmeadow et al., 2019; Johnson et al., 2014). Further work is needed pilot and 
evaluate an educational intervention that uses both factual information and techniques such as humanising cyclists to help non cyclists 
understand the legitimacy of cycling in accordance with the Highway Code.
The link between specific knowledge around cyclist positioning and specific appraisals of situations where cyclists are legitimately 
taking a centre lane position requires exploration. Further work is needed to understand specific scenarios that are perceived as ex­
amples of cyclist discourtesy. This could inform a knowledge-based intervention that focuses on where people cycling in accordance 
with the highway code are perceived as discourteous. This could work by addressing specific misconceptions about the reasons for 
certain cyclist behaviours that drivers find frustrating, by explaining why these behaviours are legitimate and rational ways to safely 
share the road with others, and not examples of cyclists being selfish, arrogant or entitled.
Recent research into humanising cyclists (Crundall and Van Loon, 2023) has shown promising results with more positive explicit 
attitudes and more cautious intended overtaking behaviour after watching a video designed to humanise a cyclist. An intervention that 
combines this humanising approach, with a demonstration of the credibility given to cyclist road positioning by the highway code, may 
be even more effective.
Not all drivers have a hostile disposition towards cyclists. Further work may focus on drivers who have been pre-selected for having 
a hostile disposition towards cyclists. Studying such a group of drivers may shed light on underlying reasons for this hostile disposition, 
including misconceptions around correct cyclist positioning, the impact of incidents where people on bicycles were not riding in 
accordance with the highway code, and rhetoric in the media and online relating to cycling.
5. Conclusions
The second stage of a hierarchical multiple regression model significantly predicted the dependent variable of cyclist discourtesy F 
(7, 147) = 6.118, p < 0.001, R2 = 0.226, with the measure of applied cyclist lane positioning knowledge adding significantly to this 
prediction (β = -0.175, t = -2.244, p = 0.026) and accounting for a significant change compared to the first stage model that excluded 
lane position knowledge (ΔR2 = 0.027, Fchange = 0.026). These results support hypothesis 1, as they show cyclist lane positioning 
knowledge predicts lower perceived cyclist discourtesy when controlling for participation in cycling and driving experience. Hy­
pothesis 2 was not supported as although the results showed there has been a significant improvement in the level of applied cyclist 
lane positioning knowledge within the group across the two stages of the survey (t(166) = -6.445, p < 0.001), a bootstrapped hier­
archical multiple regression showed the variable of highway code knowledge was not a significant predictor of lane positioning 
knowledge (F(7, 147) = 1.178, p = 0.319, R2 = 0.053).
This study has shown drivers who are better able to select suitable road positions for cyclists have lower perceptions of cyclist 
discourtesy and this effect remains significant when controlling for potential in group bias resulting from the driver’s own participation 
in cycling. There were significant improvements in participant’s ability select suitable road positions for cyclists between 2020 and 
2023, but these improvements could not be linked to specific knowledge of the Highway Code changes that formalised cyclist road 
positioning advice in 2022.
Our study provides support for exploring the benefits of improving driver knowledge of how cyclists interact with their envi­
ronment. Greater knowledge of cyclist positioning has the potential to re-frame specific interactions in a way that leads drivers towards 
a less negative interpretation of the cyclist’s behaviour. If these interactions are consistently positive, then over time they could lead to 
a more positive underlying attitude. Improving applied knowledge of how to select appropriate cyclist road positions (but not 
knowledge of the specific rules) significantly predicts lower perceptions of cyclist discourtesy. This could help reduce driver hostility 
towards cyclists, making cycling an attractive transport option for more people. This research provides the theoretical basis for 
creating and evaluating cycling-specific knowledge-based interventions for a target audience of motor vehicle drivers that has the 
potential to reduce risky overtaking manoeuvres and aggression directed towards cyclists, making cycling a more attractive transport 
option to people who are currently discouraged from doing so because of the perceived danger posed by motor vehicles.
